text: The surgeon reduces a segment of the small bowel upon entering the abdominal cavity, addressing the incarcerated inguinal hernia.
The surgeon works from medial to lateral, approaching Bogros' space, maintaining a thin dissection plane while pushing nerves and surrounding structures laterally.
The surgeon uses touch cautery to carefully apply controlled cauterization on the tissue flap, maintaining a thin layer while pulling it down.
The surgeon transitions from the flap dissection to the supravesicular space, extending the dissection medially beyond the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon identifies the indirect inguinal hernia, which is visibly lateral to the infrared epigastric vessels.
The surgeon sweeps the fat laterally, maintaining a thin flap over the nerves to ensure they remain covered, while moving from medial to lateral and vice versa.
The surgeon uses touch cautery to facilitate tissue separation, pulling down with a Maryland dissector to the left and right, applying traction and counter-traction to manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon pulls tissue up and to the left, using cautery directly on the fat to dissect down into the hernia sac. The dissection progresses with the surgeon sweeping vessels and fat laterally.
The surgeon tugs on the hernia sac, pulling it down at an oblique angle. Using a clutching motion, the hands are spun up and to the left to extract the indirect hernia sac.
The surgeon sweeps downwards, revealing a cord lipoma that comes into view. The lipoma is released and retracts back inside, indicating it will be addressed later.
The surgeon continues to sweep tissue downwards, carefully maintaining proximity to the anatomical structures to avoid encountering the vas deferens, visible on the opposite side.
The surgeon manipulates tissue at the ten o'clock position, gradually revealing anatomical structures, and then works laterally to continue the dissection.
The surgeon creates a lateral flap by sweeping the lateral fascia to cover the nerves, preparing a pocket for the mesh placement. Cautery is used to sweep down the tissue.
The surgeon observes a large hernia extending up to the deep internal ring and crossing medially across the boundary.

The surgeon carefully sweeps tissue down while maneuvering around the vas deferens and deferential vessels, aiming to maintain proximity to these structures. This technique provides a clear visualization of the iliac vein.
The surgeon meticulously dissects around the corner, gently teasing the tissue away until it drops, with the iliac vein visible in the background.

The surgeon carefully dissects and secures the tissue flap, ensuring it is large enough to accommodate the mesh without buckling. The dissection continues around the corner, providing ample space for the mesh placement.
The surgeon places two sutures for high lateral fixation on a very large indirect hernia, ensuring secure attachment of the tissue.
The surgeon positions the mesh medial to the coronamortis distal vein, ensuring secure fixation to minimize the risk of mesh migration and potential patient discomfort.
The surgeon visually identifies the muscle and proceeds to close the area using a standard one-hand technique, ensuring precise placement without blind suturing.

The surgeon continues suturing one portion of the tissue to prevent any loops, then proceeds to purse-string suture around the area to close any defects. This technique ensures the flap is securely attached, minimizing the risk of recurrence or entanglement with intestinal structures.
